briefdescription.para: \par \b Purpose\/ \: Define the abstract contract for all attachment extractors: file recognition (can_extract), extraction (extract), parameter inspection (with_attachments), and content-to-AttachedFile serialization.

detaileddescription.para: \par \b Scope\/ \: Abstract base for attachment extraction: extension/MIME matching, attachment serialization to AttachedFile list, parameter inspection. \par \b Input\/ \: file_path, extension, mime, parameters dict, binary content tuples. \par \b Output\/ \: Boolean (can_extract), List[AttachedFile] (extract, _content2attach_file). \par \b Links\/ \: [USES_API(8): dedoc.data_structures.attached_file.AttachedFile] \par \b Links\/ \: [USES_API(7): dedoc.utils.utils.get_mime_extension, dedoc.utils.utils.save_data_to_unique_file] \par \b Links\/ \: [USES_API(7): dedoc.utils.parameter_utils.get_param_attachments_dir] \par \b Invariants\/ \:

listitem.para: can_extract ALWAYS returns bool and NEVER raises.
extract is abstract; subclasses MUST implement it.

detaileddescription.para: Q: Why use ABC with abstractmethod instead of a protocol? A: extract is the core polymorphic behavior; enforcing it at instantiation time via ABC prevents runtime errors from incomplete implementations. Q: Why lazy imports inside methods? A: Avoids circular imports from dedoc.utils and reduces cold-start import overhead. \par \b Changes\/ \:
LAST_CHANGE: [v1.0.0 – Semantic markup + LDD logging added] \par \b Module Map\/ \:

CLASS 10[Abstract base contract for all attachment extractors] => AbstractAttachmentsExtractor METHOD 7[Constructor: stores config, recognized extensions/mimes] => init METHOD 8[Checks if this extractor can handle the given file] => can_extract METHOD 10[Abstract: extracts attachments from file] => extract METHOD 5[Static: checks with_attachments parameter] => with_attachments METHOD 8[Converts (name, bytes) tuples to AttachedFile list] => _content2attach_file \par \b Use Cases\/ \:
